body:
{"request_type": "chat", "response": "Your last seven days paint a picture of someone who's genuinely got their act together in some domains and is coasting in others—which is pretty honest territory to be in.

On the positive side, you've built something real with your daily exercise habit. Seven consecutive days of varied activity—runs, walks, DDR, disc golf—and your weight stayed stable despite that. That's not accident; that's a calorie balance that's working. You're also in an intense learning phase that's clearly paying off. Training almost every single day in AWS Dev and AI Engineer while prepping for interviews is serious work, and you're maintaining that discipline without burning out, which is impressive. Your gaming habits are healthily balanced too—Rocket League is your anchor but you're not mindlessly grinding; you're mixing in narrative experiences and lighter stuff.

The real tension is your food. The specialist findings were pretty direct about this: you're eating indulgently every single day, with heavy processed and fried foods as the baseline rather than the exception. The interesting bit is that despite eating substantially and often, you're staying weight-stable because your exercise is legitimate—you're basically working off what you're consuming. But here's the thing: that's efficient calorie management on paper only. You're not getting the micronutrient density or the satiety those foods typically provide, which probably means you're eating more volume than you'd need if you shifted toward more whole foods. You've got good taste—that Asian cuisine preference is a strength—and you're clearly capable of eating vegetables when they're there. The issue is they're the exception, not the rule.

So you're in an odd equilibrium: disciplined on exercise and learning, indulgent on food, balanced on gaming. You can maintain this indefinitely, but you're probably leaving energy, mental clarity, and actual enjoyment on the table by not tightening up the eating part."}
